segments.text: Hi, I'm Anirban Chatterjee. I do product
marketing here at Sonar and I'm really
excited today to take you through the
results of our state of code research
where we actually ask developers about
how they are adopting AI coding tools
and how that experience is going for
them. One thing that we've been hearing
about a lot in the industry is that the
hottest new programming language right
now is English. And it's because
developers are using English as well as
their native languages to prompt AI
tools to help them write code at a much
higher rate than they ever had before.
Even six months ago, this wasn't as much
the case as it is right now. Andre
Karpathy, the one of the former leaders
at OpenAI and currently founder of
Eureka Labs said in January of this year
that AI coding is easily the biggest
change to his basic coding workflow
in the two decades of programming that
he's done and it happened just over
the course of a few weeks. That is how
rapidly things are changing for software
engineers in this space. So where are we
right now? We know that it's no
longer a future tense statement that AI
is touching code. is actually touching
the majority of code right now whether
it's assisting developers or as an agent
working autonomously to help write
code for them in the background. We also
know based on what developers tell us
and based on experience that AI code can
be less reliable and more complex
which increases the
challenges involved in doing code review
on that AI generated code effectively.
We also know that AI tools are
generating more verbose code based on
our LLM research that one of my
colleagues took you through today.
And we also know that that more
verbose code is vastly expanding the
amount of code that needs to be reviewed
by developers. And finally, we know that
AI code despite these challenges is
already being used for mission critical
applications where quality and security
and maintainability are of paramount
importance. So what does that mean? How
is that it actually going? with
developers right now as they adopt this
brand new technology as part of their
workflow. This is what the state of code
developer survey report is all about.
is one of the most widely
adopted solutions for code quality
around the world and we've already
published a number of reports exploring
this space over the course of the past
six months or so. Looking at code
reliability, security, maintainability
that we see in and some of the
coding styles that leading LLMs are
exhibiting. This is actually research
that we update on an ongoing basis. But
we also wanted to couple that
database data exploration with actual
research of asking developers how their
experiences are going. And so what we
did with this survey report is we
surveyed developers writing code and
collaborating with with AI to write code
to find out what their experience is
like and how it's been evolving over
time. If you want to read any of
these reports in detail, you can go to
the URL on the screen,
sonarsource.com/research
to get not only this report but every
other research report that we've done at
sonar. But for this report specifically,
it was a quantitative survey done online
where we actually went to
professional software developers
throughout the month of October of last
year. The final sample size included
just over 1,000 people distributed
globally and the responses went
across a spectrum of countries and
seniority levels junior and senior
developers as well as different company
sizes like SMBs and enterprises and
everybody in between. We also made sure
to ensure that every
responder was 18 years or older was
employed either full-time or
self-employed in a technology role.
most of these folks worked in software
engineering and that they were either
writing code actively as part of the
role or managing developers writing code
using at least one programming
language that we care about at
Sonar. I'm going to summarize the top
five findings that we found in this
survey. As I mentioned, you can go get
all of the findings from the survey
download site I mentioned earlier. But
let's start with the first finding,
which is that 72% of developers who have
tried AI use it nearly every day, which
is a big stat. I was expecting it to
be 50 or 60%, 72% was a surprise to me.
The interesting thing about this is that
they're using it not just for
prototyping and experimentation, which
is what the the vast majority of usage
was maybe even a year ago. They have
actually progressed well beyond that to
58% of developers telling us they're
using it for production software for
business critical and mission critical
services. There's also a wide variety of
use cases that developers are using
AI tools for. You'll see a whole list of
use cases that we asked about on the
left-hand side of your screen right now
as well as a cluster of dots that show
not only how often developers tell us
they use AI for that use case, but how
effective they believe the AI tool
was for that use case. I want to call
out two particular use cases
specifically. The first one a writing
documentation. This is the use case that
developers felt was most effective about
75% effective rate for AI to write
documentation. However, that wasn't the
most often used use case. That honor
falls to assisting development of new
code. Basically most developers about
nine out of 10 developers told us they
are using AI tools to assist development
of new code even though they believe it
is only a little over 50% effective at
that task which is interesting. The
second big finding from this survey 96%
of developers don't fully trust that AI
generated code is functionally correct.
Now if you look at the pie chart here
you'll see that we have 4% of responders
telling us that they completely agree
with the statement that they trust AI
code is functionally correct. This means
that 96% of folks at least have some
level of doubt about whether that code
coming out of the AI tool is
functionally correct or not. However,
only 48% of developers are actively
always checking the code coming out. So
you have this situation
where 96% of developers don't fully
trust the output, but less than half of
those developers telling us that they
always run that through a code review
process before checking it in, which was
surprising to me. The third set of
findings from our survey are all about
the top tools being used by developers
to write AI generated code. As you can
see, the top tools on the list here
are GitHub copilot and chatgpt which are
no surprise to anybody. Following right
behind them are Claude and Cloud Code,
Gemini and Cursor. And then the rest of
the list are just other tools that we
asked about with the bottom row others
is just a collection of all of the other
tools that we asked about along with
tools that were written in by developers
as part of the survey. GitHub Copilot
being in the top list is no surprise
because many of the developers that we
speak to, especially in the professional
sense are GitHub customers. They're
basically using GitHub as part of
writing and and shipping code in their
work and so Copilot being available to
them just makes it an obvious move to
use copilot as part of code development.
GPT is also an extremely popular tool
for a number of reasons including
generating code and cloud code has
become very popular lately to write
code. The interesting thing for me
though is how these tools are actually
being accessed by all the different
software engineers that we asked. So
what's interesting is for Perplexity as
an example, the majority of developers
using Perplexity as part of the
professional work were accessing it
through a personal account. With
ChatGpt, it was about 50/50 with half of
developers telling us they use it
through a work section account and half
using it through a personal account. No
surprise, Copilot is at the bottom of
the screen here showing that 78% of
developers were accessing it through
a work section account whereas only 17%
are accessing it through a personal
account. And Amazon is right there at
the same fraction of work section
versus personal account. The surprising
thing was that if you take all of the
top 10 tools and average them together,
about 35% of the usage from developers,
we're using them via personal accounts,
which is an interesting finding and
probably something that would be
concerning to a CTO or a CIO type person
who is trying to make sure that
developers are using approved tools in a
safe way and not inadvertently leaking
information that's not
firewalled properly through a corporate
account. And so that's something that
we'll be paying more attention to as we
go forward because I fully expect that
as we get further into 2026 and beyond,
the number of tools that are available
for developers to use will start to
consolidate and we'll start to pick some
winners and losers and have fewer
numbers of tools that developers are
using at one time because right now
developers are using on average four
different tools as part of professional
work to write code. There's probably one
or two that are work sanctioned and
there are others that are being used
through a personal account for them to
do their jobs. The fourth set of
findings from our survey is all about
developer toil which is an area that we
know is interesting not only to
developers but also to their leaders. We
asked developers if they believed AI
tools help them reduce the toil in their
workday and 75% of them told us that AI
does help them reduce their toil. But
the interesting thing is it didn't
actually. We also asked developers to
tell us what they actually spend their
time on during the workday. But we gave
them a whole list of tasks and we asked
them to assign percentages to all of
them. And so we know where they spent
their workday, whether it was a toilet
task or not. And when we added up the
amount of time they actually spent on
toil work, we found that it was around
25%. And the interesting thing is we
actually separated the group of
developers into a group of frequent AI
users and a group of developers who
didn't use AI as frequently. And the
amount of time that they both spent on
Tolwork independently was about the same
about a quarter of their time. You'll
see that there's a blue curve and a red
curve where the red curve is users who
are using AI frequently and the blue
curve is users who are using AI less
frequently and they're nearly on top of
each other. So what that's telling us is
that AI is not so much reducing the toil
that developers are experiencing, but
it's changing its shape, changing the
way they actually spend their workday
and just moving the toil from some tasks
to others. We actually asked about some
of these tasks, which is what this next
chart is about. And you'll see here we
have a dark blue bar that shows how much
developers told us they spent time on
that toil task. The dark blue bar being
de users who are using AI multiple times
per day. And then the light blue bars
indicate developers who are using AI
weekly or occasionally, so a little bit
less often. And so you'll see we have
different levels of people assigning
their day to that particular toil task
whether they were using AI frequently or
not. A couple things I want to call out
on this slide. First of all, the two
rows with red bars, those are areas
showing that AI actually increased toil
in those particular areas. specifically
managing tech debt which we know is
something that AI can potentially do if
it's not controlled effectively because
it'll add verbosity add complexity to
code if it's not managed properly. So
it's no surprise that people who are
using AI frequently are spending more
time managing the tech debt that is
resulting from using AI. The the fourth
one on the list here correcting or
rewriting code created by AI coding
tools while obviously developers who are
using AI frequently are going to spend
more time on that kind of toilet work.
The two rows with blue arrows on the
other hand, these indicate areas where
developers who use AI less frequently
experience more toil than developers
using AI more frequently. The these are
areas where we believe AI is actually
helping developers be more effective at
those tasks. For example, debugging
legacy or poorly documented code. This
is an area where we know AI can be
helpful in helping especially junior
developers find their way in a new
codebase and also finding information or
understanding existing systems. Again,
help helping understand what's happening
in a codebase that you're maybe less
familiar with is something that AI
excels at and is one of the use cases
that have been called out for AI is
working particularly well. So, it's no
surprise that developers who use AI
frequently are spending less time on
toil in those areas. One thing that has
come up over and over again is that when
you want to use AI effectively, you want
to be able to put guard rails on how
that AI is used and how the AI is
actually optimized for your workflow and
also for your organization. Those
guardrails can take multiple forms, but
one of the most effective guardrails
that we have found is actually using
to control quality in a
codebase and using to make
sure that as AI code is getting
generated, it is being evaluated in a
consistent way in a way that is the same
throughout the entire organization so
that developers have a little bit of
help in taking the massive amount of
code, the volume of code that is being
generated by AI coding tools and running
it through a regime of automated and
manual code review that is assisted by
Sonar. And so it's one thing that
we found when we looked at
users in our survey versus non-
users in our survey is that
users are seeing better success
metrics more often than non-
users when they are adopting AI. They're
basically getting more ROI faster out of
AI coding tools than users who are not
benefiting from having
available to them. For example, we find
that there are users
reporting lower vulnerability rates in
AI-generated code 24% more often than
non-users. They're also reporting
lower defect rates 20% more often than
non-users. Lower rework and patch
costs 18% more often than non-
users. Lower tech debt 16% more often.
And higher overall code quality 12% more
often than non-users. This is
something that we're going to continue
to evaluate as we do more versions of
this survey. But we feel pretty
confident in saying that
users are actually getting more ROI from
AI than non users because of the
guard rails that allow them to have
consistent standards of quality
throughout the entire codebase, whether
the code is being written by a developer
that's human or a developer that's
powered by an AI. Those are all the
findings we're going to cover today. If
you have any questions, we'd love to
hear from you. There's chat rooms
available in this event that you can
join and ask questions about the survey
if you like. I also highly recommend
that you go to our website that I went
through earlier,

if you want to download the full report
sonarsource.com/research,
along with any of the research that
we've done. Thank you very much for
joining me today.
